metadata.context: General evaluation guidelines for Toronto civic and public infrastructure: parks, libraries, community centres, transportation, and hospitals. Covers finance, access, safety, staffing, equity, community integration, and more.

chunks.text:
This evaluation framework provides general guidelines for assessing Toronto civic and public infrastructure facilities, including parks, public libraries, community centres, transit infrastructure, and hospitals/healthcare facilities. The framework is designed to be reusable across building evaluations without regenerating a new document for each site.

The framework draws on Toronto's civic accountability ecosystem: the City of Toronto's Service Equity policies, Toronto Public Health population health profiles, the Toronto Public Library accountability reports, Toronto Parks Forestry and Recreation divisional reports, TTC and Metrolinx performance frameworks, and Ontario Ministry of Health/CIHI guidelines for acute-care facilities.

Core evaluation principles applicable to all facility types:
1. Equity-first lens: Toronto's population is highly diverse; any facility evaluation must assess access and outcomes across income quintiles, racialized groups, language groups, and disability status.
2. Neighbourhood context: use Toronto Public Health neighbourhood profiles and the Neighbourhood Equity Score (NES) to contextualize utilization and outcomes for each site.
3. Data triangulation: combine quantitative indicators (counts, rates, ratios) with qualitative signals (community surveys, complaints, engagement maturity).
4. Peer benchmarking: compare facilities against similar Toronto sites (same facility type and neighbourhood tier) rather than city-wide averages alone.
5. Trend over time: point-in-time scores are less meaningful than directional improvement; report at minimum a 3-year trend for every indicator.
6. Proxy guidance: many ideal metrics are not publicly reported; for each indicator this framework identifies the best available proxy where primary data are unavailable.

Benchmarks across this framework are derived using one of five methods, always stated explicitly for each metric:

1. Legislated or regulatory standard: a mandatory minimum set in law, building code, or provincial/municipal regulation (e.g., Ontario Building Code clearances, AODA accessibility requirements, Ontario Fire Code inspection intervals). Good = compliance; Poor = non-compliance.

2. City of Toronto service standard: an adopted target in a divisional service plan or Toronto Council-approved policy (e.g., parkland per capita targets, library branch catchment area standards). Good = meets or exceeds standard; Sufficient = within 10% of standard; Poor = >10% below standard.

3. Provincial or national benchmark: published targets from Ontario ministries, Ontario Health, CIHI, or Statistics Canada (e.g., ED wait-time targets, transit on-time performance targets from Metrolinx). Good = at or above benchmark; Poor = materially below.

4. Peer distribution: where no fixed target exists, rank the facility against a defined peer group (same facility type, comparable catchment population size, comparable neighbourhood equity score). Good = top quartile; Sufficient = middle two quartiles; Poor = bottom quartile — always accompanied by statistical confidence.

5. Evidence-based operational threshold: thresholds derived from published research or professional standards (e.g., 85% occupancy as a capacity stress threshold, ASHRAE ventilation standards, CPTED design principles). Good/Poor defined per indicator based on the underlying evidence.

Each facility is scored on a 0–100 scale per metric, aggregated to domain scores, then to an overall facility score.

Metric normalization:
- Target-based metrics (compliance %, on-time %): score = (actual / target) × 100, capped at 100.
- 'Lower is better' rates (incident rates, complaint rates): score = 100 × (benchmark / actual) if actual > benchmark, else 100.
- Peer-distribution metrics: top quartile = 75–100; second quartile = 50–74; third = 25–49; bottom = 0–24.
- Maturity/qualitative rubrics (0–4 scale): linearly rescaled to 0–100.

Domain weighting (three recommended scenarios — always run sensitivity):
- Balanced: equal weight across all applicable domains (1/N).
- Safety-first: double weight on Safety & Compliance and Physical Condition; reduce weight on Finance and Community Engagement.
- Equity-first: double weight on Equity & Inclusion and Community Access; reflect Toronto's service equity mandate.

Missing data rules:
- Not reported due to low volume or privacy: flag as 'insufficient evidence'; use 3-year rolling average where possible.
- Service not applicable to this facility type: remove metric from denominator and re-normalize.
- Non-reporting/compliance failure: apply explicit penalty flag; do not score as average.

Sensitivity analysis: vary each domain weight ±20% and report how overall rank changes. Report scores under all three weighting scenarios. Flag any metric with small denominators as 'high uncertainty.'

Domain: Finance and fiscal sustainability
Metric: Operating cost per visit / per user
Definition: Total annual operating expenditure ÷ total annual visits (or registered users). Unit: $ per visit.
Rationale: Core efficiency signal; tracks cost to deliver the service per unit of use; enables peer comparison across similar facility types.
Data sources: City of Toronto divisional budget submissions; annual service performance reports; Toronto Open Data expenditure datasets.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Benchmark via peer group distribution (same facility type and tier). Good: ≤25th percentile cost vs peer group. Poor: ≥75th percentile after confirming comparable scope and service mix.
Availability/proxy: Often available via City budget transparency portal. Proxy: total divisional spend per square metre if visit data unavailable.

---

Domain: Finance and fiscal sustainability
Metric: Capital reinvestment rate
Definition: Annual capital expenditure on facility ÷ estimated replacement value of facility assets. Unit: %.
Rationale: Sustained under-investment creates deferred maintenance backlog, safety risk, and accelerating future costs.
Data sources: City of Toronto Capital Budget; State of Good Repair (SOGR) backlog reports published by City divisions.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Evidence-based threshold: Good: ≥2% per year (facility management convention); Sufficient: 1–2%; Poor: <1% sustained.
Availability/proxy: SOGR backlog data is published by the City for major asset classes. Proxy: age of major building systems vs expected service life.

---

Domain: Finance and fiscal sustainability
Metric: State of Good Repair (SOGR) backlog as % of replacement value
Definition: Cumulative deferred maintenance cost ÷ current replacement value of facility. Unit: %.
Rationale: Directly measures infrastructure risk; Toronto City Council tracks SOGR backlog as a system-level fiscal risk.
Data sources: City of Toronto Asset Management Plan; divisional SOGR reports; Toronto's 10-Year Capital Plan.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: <5% (low risk). Fair: 5–10%. Poor: >10% (high risk, approaching crisis). Benchmark derived from City of Toronto asset management conventions and engineering facility management practice.
Availability/proxy: Published at asset-class level; may not be facility-specific. Proxy: condition audit score from most recent facility inspection.

---

Domain: Finance and fiscal sustainability
Metric: Revenue diversification ratio
Definition: Non-tax / non-levy revenue (fees, grants, partnerships, rentals) ÷ total operating revenue. Unit: %.
Rationale: Higher diversification reduces fiscal vulnerability; relevant for libraries, community centres, and hospitals with fee structures.
Data sources: Facility annual report; divisional budget; City of Toronto budget variance reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: No universal target; benchmark via peer group distribution and trend. Good: improving ratio without compromising access equity.
Availability/proxy: Partially public via budget documents. Proxy: grant income and partnership revenue listed in annual reports.

---

Domain: Finance and fiscal sustainability
Metric: Budget variance (actual vs approved)
Definition: (Actual expenditure − approved budget) ÷ approved budget. Unit: %.
Rationale: Material and persistent variances signal planning, procurement, or operational issues.
Data sources: City of Toronto budget variance reports; divisional year-end reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: within ±5% of approved budget. Poor: >10% over or under in consecutive years without approved plan.
Availability/proxy: Published in City budget cycle documents.

Domain: Access and availability
Metric: Geographic catchment coverage (% of residents within service-standard distance)
Definition: % of Toronto residents within the City's adopted service-standard travel distance/time to the nearest facility of this type. Unit: %.
Rationale: Core spatial equity signal; Toronto has adopted distance standards for parks, libraries, and community centres.
Data sources: City of Toronto GIS / Open Data; divisional service standards (e.g., 500m to a park, 1.2km to a library branch).
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥90% of catchment population within standard. Poor: <75%. Derived from City of Toronto adopted service standards.
Availability/proxy: Computable from Toronto Open Data facility locations and census population data. Proxy: % of neighbourhoods with NES <40 that are underserved.

---

Domain: Access and availability
Metric: Hours of operation vs community need
Definition: Total weekly open hours; % of hours during evenings and weekends (when working residents can access). Unit: hours/week; %.
Rationale: Facilities open only 9–5 weekdays exclude working-age adults and families; Toronto service equity lens emphasizes evening/weekend access.
Data sources: Facility operating schedule; Toronto Recreation / TPL branch hours databases.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥40% of hours outside 9–5 weekday window. Poor: <20% of hours outside standard business hours. Derived from equity-access principle.
Availability/proxy: Public via facility websites and Toronto Open Data.

---

Domain: Access and availability
Metric: AODA / accessibility compliance score
Definition: % of AODA (Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act) requirements met: physical access, accessible programming, digital access, staff training. Unit: %.
Rationale: Legal obligation under Ontario law; compliance directly determines whether people with disabilities can use the facility.
Data sources: City of Toronto Multi-Year Accessibility Plan; facility-level accessibility audit reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: 100% compliance (legal standard). Poor: any non-compliance with mandatory requirements. Use phased-in deadlines where applicable.
Availability/proxy: City of Toronto publishes AODA accessibility reports. Proxy: count of barriers identified in last audit that remain unresolved.

---

Domain: Access and availability
Metric: Waitlist / unmet demand rate
Definition: % of program registrations or service requests that go unfulfilled due to capacity constraints. Unit: %.
Rationale: Waitlists are the clearest signal of unmet community demand; important for community centres, subsidized programs, and library holds.
Data sources: Facility registration systems (ActiveTO, Toronto Public Library catalogue holds); divisional service reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: <10% unmet demand. Poor: >25% unmet demand sustained over two consecutive years.
Availability/proxy: Partially available through City service performance reports. Proxy: program fill rate and cancellation-due-to-low-registration rate.

---

Domain: Access and availability
Metric: Fee assistance / subsidy uptake rate
Definition: # residents using fee subsidy programs (e.g., Toronto's Recreation Fee Assistance, Access to Toronto Public Library) ÷ estimated eligible population. Unit: %.
Rationale: Low uptake of subsidy programs in high-need neighbourhoods signals access barriers beyond cost (awareness, stigma, registration friction).
Data sources: City of Toronto Recreation Fee Assistance program data; TPL equity access data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: uptake rate rising year-over-year in NES <40 neighbourhoods. Poor: flat or declining uptake in high-need areas despite program availability.
Availability/proxy: Partially public in divisional annual reports. Proxy: registration rates for free vs fee-based programming by ward.

Domain: Physical condition and infrastructure
Metric: Facility condition index (FCI)
Definition: Deferred maintenance backlog ÷ current replacement value. Unit: %.
Rationale: Standard industry measure of infrastructure risk; directly affects safety, reliability, and service continuity.
Data sources: City of Toronto Facilities Management condition audits; divisional SOGR reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: <5% (low risk). Fair: 5–10%. Poor: >10% (high risk). Engineering convention confirmed in City of Toronto asset management guidance.
Availability/proxy: Often not facility-specific in public reporting. Proxy: age of HVAC, roof, and structural systems relative to expected service life; number of outstanding work orders >90 days.

---

Domain: Physical condition and infrastructure
Metric: Unplanned facility closure rate
Definition: # unplanned closures per year (mechanical failure, flooding, HVAC, pest, safety) ÷ total operating days. Unit: rate per 100 operating days.
Rationale: Unplanned closures directly disrupt service access and signal underlying infrastructure deficiency.
Data sources: Facility incident logs; 311 service request data (Toronto Open Data); divisional operational reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: <1 unplanned closure per 100 operating days. Poor: >3 per 100 operating days or any closure due to life-safety hazard.
Availability/proxy: 311 data available on Toronto Open Data. Proxy: insurance claims and emergency repair expenditures.

---

Domain: Physical condition and infrastructure
Metric: Indoor environmental quality (IEQ) compliance
Definition: % of most recent air quality, ventilation (ASHRAE 62.1), thermal comfort, and lighting assessments passing standard thresholds. Unit: %.
Rationale: Poor IEQ affects health outcomes for users and staff; critical for buildings with vulnerable populations (children, elderly, immunocompromised).
Data sources: Toronto Public Health building inspection records; facility environmental audits; HVAC maintenance logs.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: 100% pass on mandatory standards. Poor: any active health-relevant exceedance (CO2 >1000ppm sustained, mold, legionella risk).
Availability/proxy: Toronto Public Health inspection results are publicly available. Proxy: HVAC filter replacement compliance and ventilation maintenance records.

---

Domain: Physical condition and infrastructure
Metric: Energy use intensity (EUI)
Definition: Total energy consumption (electricity + natural gas) ÷ gross floor area. Unit: ekWh/m²/year.
Rationale: Energy efficiency signals infrastructure quality and supports Toronto's climate commitments (TransformTO Net Zero Strategy); also a direct operating cost driver.
Data sources: Toronto's Energy Benchmarking reporting (required for buildings >50,000 sq ft under Toronto Green Standard); Toronto Hydro and Enbridge data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Benchmark via building type (CBECS peer group or NRCan PortfolioManager benchmarks). Good: bottom quartile EUI vs peer type. Poor: top quartile EUI with no improvement trend.
Availability/proxy: Toronto Energy Benchmarking data is published annually on Toronto Open Data for covered buildings.

---

Domain: Physical condition and infrastructure
Metric: Digital infrastructure readiness
Definition: Availability of public WiFi, accessible digital terminals, and current security/IT systems. Unit: binary checklist + uptime %.
Rationale: Digital access is a basic equity requirement; outdated IT infrastructure limits programming and community access.
Data sources: Facility IT asset inventory; Toronto Public Library digital access reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: public WiFi available throughout public areas with ≥99% uptime; all digital terminals accessible per AODA. Poor: no public WiFi or >5% unplanned downtime.
Availability/proxy: TPL publishes digital access metrics. Proxy: age of public-facing IT hardware and last network upgrade date.

Domain: Safety and compliance
Metric: Ontario Fire Code compliance (inspection pass rate)
Definition: % of items passing most recent Toronto Fire Services inspection, including fire suppression, egress, alarms, and extinguisher maintenance. Unit: %.
Rationale: Legal requirement under Ontario Fire Protection and Prevention Act; non-compliance creates direct life-safety risk.
Data sources: Toronto Fire Services inspection records (available via Freedom of Information or open data portals).
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: 100% pass (or all deficiencies corrected within required timelines). Poor: outstanding life-safety deficiencies beyond required correction period.
Availability/proxy: Toronto Fire Services publishes summary inspection data. Proxy: time since last inspection vs required frequency.

---

Domain: Safety and compliance
Metric: Ontario Building Code compliance status
Definition: Active orders, outstanding violations, or permit non-compliance under the Ontario Building Code. Unit: count of outstanding orders.
Rationale: Building Code sets minimum structural, life-safety, and accessibility standards; outstanding orders represent unmitigated legal and safety risk.
Data sources: City of Toronto Building Division permit and inspection records (Toronto Open Data).
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: zero outstanding orders. Poor: any active Order to Comply or Stop Work Order unresolved beyond prescribed timeline.
Availability/proxy: Permit records available on Toronto Open Data. Proxy: age of facility without documented major permit/inspection history.

---

Domain: Safety and compliance
Metric: Reported safety incidents per 10,000 visits
Definition: Total reported safety incidents (injuries, near-misses, security incidents) ÷ total visits × 10,000. Unit: rate.
Rationale: Captures operational safety performance; rising rates signal management, design, or maintenance failures.
Data sources: Facility incident management logs; WSIB claims (if staff-related); City risk management reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Benchmark via peer group distribution and trend. Good: rate below peer median and declining. Poor: rate above peer 75th percentile or rising trend.
Availability/proxy: Partially disclosed in divisional reports. Proxy: 311 safety-related service requests and insurance claims by facility.

---

Domain: Safety and compliance
Metric: Workplace health and safety (WSIB lost-time injury rate)
Definition: Lost-time injuries per 100 FTE workers per year. Unit: rate.
Rationale: Staff safety is a quality and sustainability prerequisite; injury rates drive absenteeism, overtime, and worker retention.
Data sources: WSIB sector data; facility HR reporting.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: below Ontario municipal sector average for facility type. Poor: above sector average and worsening trend.
Availability/proxy: WSIB publishes sector averages. Proxy: sick time rate and modified work program usage.

---

Domain: Safety and compliance
Metric: Security and personal safety perception score
Definition: % of users rating the facility as 'safe' or 'very safe' in community satisfaction surveys. Unit: %.
Rationale: Perceived safety determines whether community members — especially women, youth, and marginalized groups — will use the facility.
Data sources: City of Toronto community satisfaction surveys; facility-specific user surveys.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥85% rate facility as safe. Poor: <70% — triggers mandatory safety audit and CPTED (Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design) review.
Availability/proxy: City satisfaction survey data partially public. Proxy: 311 public safety complaints and Toronto Police Service calls for service by location.

Domain: Staffing and human resources
Metric: Staff-to-user ratio (by facility type)
Definition: Frontline staff FTE ÷ average daily users. Unit: staff per 100 users. Benchmarks differ by facility type (see facility-specific sections).
Rationale: Directly affects service quality, safety supervision, and user experience; must be adjusted for program type and user vulnerability.
Data sources: Divisional staffing plans; City of Toronto HR data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Benchmark via peer group and professional standards for each facility type. Good: at or above minimum professional standard and peer median. Poor: sustained below minimum standard.
Availability/proxy: Staff counts in divisional reports. Proxy: program cancellation rate due to insufficient staff.

---

Domain: Staffing and human resources
Metric: Staff vacancy and turnover rate
Definition: Vacancy rate: % of authorized positions unfilled. Turnover: # separations ÷ average headcount × 100. Unit: %.
Rationale: High vacancy and turnover destabilize service quality, erode institutional knowledge, and increase overtime costs.
Data sources: City of Toronto HR reporting; divisional workforce plans.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: vacancy <5%; turnover <15% annually. Poor: vacancy >15% sustained or turnover >25%. Thresholds derived from public sector workforce benchmarks.
Availability/proxy: Partially disclosed in City budget notes. Proxy: # of postings open >60 days on City careers portal.

---

Domain: Staffing and human resources
Metric: Workforce diversity and equity representation
Definition: % of staff (and leadership) from equity-deserving groups vs % of catchment population from those groups. Unit: representation gap (%).
Rationale: Toronto's equity mandate requires that service workforces reflect the communities they serve; representational gaps affect cultural competency and community trust.
Data sources: City of Toronto Workforce Census (published every 3 years); divisional equity plans.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: representation gap <5% for equity-deserving groups at all levels. Poor: >15% gap at frontline or leadership, not improving.
Availability/proxy: City Workforce Census data published on City website. Proxy: language competency of frontline staff vs top 5 languages in catchment.

---

Domain: Staffing and human resources
Metric: Staff training compliance (mandatory training completion rate)
Definition: % of staff current on mandatory training: First Aid/CPR, WHMIS, AODA, Anti-Black Racism / equity training, and facility-type-specific certifications. Unit: %.
Rationale: Mandatory training compliance is a legal requirement and quality prerequisite; gaps create liability and service quality risk.
Data sources: City of Toronto Learning Management System (LMS) records; divisional HR reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥95% current on all mandatory training. Poor: <85% or any staff operating without required certification (e.g., lifeguard certification at aquatic facilities).
Availability/proxy: Internal HR data; proxy: accreditation status and any regulatory orders related to unqualified staff.

---

Domain: Staffing and human resources
Metric: Overtime and casual/contract reliance rate
Definition: Overtime hours ÷ total worked hours; # casual/contract FTE ÷ total FTE. Unit: %.
Rationale: High overtime and casual reliance signal structural understaffing; also creates service continuity and quality risk.
Data sources: City of Toronto payroll data; divisional budget variance reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: overtime <5% of total hours; casual/contract <20% of FTE. Poor: overtime >10% sustained or casual >35% — triggers workforce planning review.
Availability/proxy: Disclosed in budget variance reports. Proxy: program cancellations attributed to staffing shortages.

Domain: Equity and inclusion
Metric: Neighbourhood Equity Score (NES) alignment
Definition: Cross-reference of facility quality/investment scores against Toronto's Neighbourhood Equity Score for the facility's catchment area. Higher NES = less disadvantaged; lower NES = more equity need.
Rationale: Toronto's equity framework requires that facilities serving lower-NES neighbourhoods receive proportionally higher investment and programming focus.
Data sources: City of Toronto Neighbourhood Equity Score (published on Toronto Open Data); facility catchment boundary mapping.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: facilities in NES <40 neighbourhoods score ≥ city average on access and condition metrics. Poor: facilities in lowest-NES neighbourhoods consistently score below city average.
Availability/proxy: NES data publicly available. Proxy: per-capita investment differential between highest- and lowest-NES facility catchments.

---

Domain: Equity and inclusion
Metric: Multilingual service provision
Definition: % of service touchpoints (signage, website, programming, front-desk staff) available in the top 5 languages spoken in the facility's catchment area. Unit: %.
Rationale: Toronto is the most linguistically diverse city in North America; language barriers are the #1 reported access barrier in City user surveys.
Data sources: Statistics Canada Census language data by FSA/neighbourhood; facility service audit.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥80% of touchpoints available in top 5 catchment languages. Poor: <50% or critical safety communications not available in dominant non-English languages.
Availability/proxy: Census language data publicly available. Proxy: availability of translated materials and language line access at reception.

---

Domain: Equity and inclusion
Metric: Indigenous cultural safety
Definition: Presence of Indigenous cultural programming, Indigenous Staff/Elder relationships, land acknowledgment practices, and patient/user-reported cultural safety scores. Unit: checklist + score.
Rationale: Toronto is home to Indigenous peoples from many nations; facilities must move beyond performative acknowledgment to structural cultural safety.
Data sources: Divisional Indigenous affairs plans; facility program schedules; user surveys disaggregated by Indigenous identity.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Must be co-defined with Indigenous partners and communities. Good: documented partnership with Indigenous organizations + improving user-reported cultural safety. Poor: no structural Indigenous engagement beyond land acknowledgment.
Availability/proxy: Rarely public in detail. Proxy: Indigenous-specific programming hours and documented partnership agreements.

---

Domain: Equity and inclusion
Metric: Gender-inclusive facilities
Definition: Availability of gender-neutral washrooms, change rooms, and programming. Unit: binary + count.
Rationale: Toronto's commitment to LGBTQ2S+ inclusion requires gender-neutral infrastructure in all public facilities; absence creates exclusion and safety risk.
Data sources: Facility physical audit; City of Toronto LGBTQ2S+ inclusion audits.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: at least one gender-neutral washroom and change room accessible per public floor. Poor: none available anywhere in facility.
Availability/proxy: Facility audit required. Proxy: user complaints related to washroom/change room access.

---

Domain: Equity and inclusion
Metric: Program equity: subsidized and low-barrier program proportion
Definition: % of total programming slots that are free, subsidized, or accessible via fee-assistance. Unit: %.
Rationale: Ensures the facility's programming mix reflects the access needs of its catchment population, not just paying users.
Data sources: Facility program schedule and fee structure; City Recreation fee assistance data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥30% of programming slots free or subsidized in NES <50 catchments. Poor: <15% in high-need catchments.
Availability/proxy: Program schedules are public. Proxy: ratio of fee-waiver registrations to total registrations.

Domain: Community engagement and user experience
Metric: User satisfaction score
Definition: % of users rating overall experience as 'satisfied' or 'very satisfied' in standardized surveys. Unit: %.
Rationale: Global experience signal; Toronto conducts resident satisfaction surveys across most major service divisions.
Data sources: City of Toronto Resident and Business Survey; divisional user experience surveys.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥80% satisfied. Poor: <65%. Peer benchmark within facility type. Ensure survey methodology captures diverse users, not just frequent/self-selecting ones.
Availability/proxy: City publishes satisfaction data by service category. Proxy: Google Reviews / public feedback aggregators (triangulate, not primary source).

---

Domain: Community engagement and user experience
Metric: Formal complaints rate and resolution time
Definition: Formal complaints per 10,000 visits; median days to resolution. Unit: rate; days.
Rationale: Complaints capture service failures invisible in administrative data; resolution time signals organizational responsiveness.
Data sources: 311 service request data (Toronto Open Data); facility complaints log.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: complaint rate <5 per 10,000 visits and median resolution ≤15 business days. Poor: rate >15 per 10,000 or median resolution >30 days.
Availability/proxy: 311 data published on Toronto Open Data by facility address. Proxy: social media complaints and Yelp/Google Review themes.

---

Domain: Community engagement and user experience
Metric: Community co-design maturity
Definition: Qualitative rubric: 0 = no engagement; 1 = information only; 2 = consultation; 3 = co-design with community; 4 = community-led governance with staff support. Unit: maturity level 0–4.
Rationale: Facilities designed and operated with community co-ownership are more responsive, more equitable, and more resilient.
Data sources: Facility governance documents; community advisory committee records; City Clerk's community consultation records.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥3 (co-design embedded in capital and programming decisions). Poor: ≤1 (token consultation or none).
Availability/proxy: Governance documents partially public. Proxy: presence and activity of community advisory group; last date of public programming consultation.

---

Domain: Community engagement and user experience
Metric: Visit/utilization rate vs capacity
Definition: Actual annual visits ÷ design capacity visits (hours × max users per session × operating days). Unit: %.
Rationale: Under-utilization may signal access barriers, poor programming fit, or inadequate marketing; over-utilization signals capacity constraint and potential exclusion.
Data sources: Facility visit counters; registration system data; Toronto Open Data where published.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: 60–85% utilization. Poor: <40% (severe underuse — investigate barriers) or >95% sustained (severe crowding — capacity investment needed).
Availability/proxy: Some facilities report via Toronto Open Data. Proxy: room booking occupancy rates and waitlist length.

---

Domain: Community engagement and user experience
Metric: Programming diversity index
Definition: Proportion of programming that addresses each of: fitness/recreation, cultural, educational, social services, seniors, youth, children, accessible/adapted. Unit: entropy index or % per category.
Rationale: Programming diversity ensures the facility serves its whole catchment, not a narrow demographic; important for community centres and libraries.
Data sources: Facility program schedule; registration data by program type.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥6 of 8 programming categories active and proportionate to catchment demographics. Poor: ≤3 categories, with over-representation of fee-generating programs.
Availability/proxy: Program schedules are public. Proxy: comparison of program categories vs top community needs identified in most recent ward-level needs assessment.

Domain: Environmental sustainability
Metric: GHG emissions intensity
Definition: Total greenhouse gas emissions (Scope 1 + 2) ÷ gross floor area. Unit: kgCO2e/m²/year.
Rationale: Toronto's TransformTO Net Zero by 2040 strategy requires city-owned buildings to progressively decarbonize; emissions intensity is the primary tracking metric.
Data sources: Toronto's Energy Benchmarking program (open data); Toronto Hydro; Enbridge consumption data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Benchmark via NRCan PortfolioManager Canadian building type averages. Good: below sector median and improving. Poor: above sector median with no decarbonization plan.
Availability/proxy: Published on Toronto Open Data for buildings >50,000 sq ft. Proxy: energy audit date and retrofit plan status.

---

Domain: Environmental sustainability
Metric: Waste diversion rate
Definition: % of facility waste diverted from landfill (recycling + organics + reuse). Unit: %.
Rationale: Toronto's long-term waste management strategy includes targets for all City facilities; poor diversion signals operational and programming gaps.
Data sources: City of Toronto Solid Waste Management Services facility audit data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥70% diversion (Toronto residential target context). Poor: <50%. Derived from City of Toronto waste strategy goals.
Availability/proxy: Divisional environmental reports. Proxy: presence of organics collection and multi-stream recycling throughout facility.

---

Domain: Environmental sustainability
Metric: Green space and tree canopy contribution (parks and outdoor facilities)
Definition: % of site that is permeable surface or tree canopy. Unit: %.
Rationale: Permeable surfaces and tree canopy reduce urban heat island effects, manage stormwater, and support biodiversity — core goals of Toronto's Urban Forest Strategy and Wet Weather Flow Master Plan.
Data sources: Toronto Urban Forest inventory (open data); City GIS impervious surface mapping.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥40% permeable/canopy on site. Poor: <20% — particularly critical for facilities in high urban heat island areas.
Availability/proxy: Toronto tree canopy data on Open Data. Proxy: heat vulnerability index score for facility neighbourhood (Toronto Public Health).

---

Domain: Environmental sustainability
Metric: Climate resilience (flood and extreme heat risk)
Definition: Whether facility has undergone climate risk assessment and has documented flood/extreme heat resilience plan. Unit: binary + risk tier.
Rationale: Toronto faces increasing flood and extreme heat risk; City facilities must be resilient hubs, especially for vulnerable populations.
Data sources: City of Toronto climate risk assessments; Toronto and Region Conservation Authority (TRCA) flood plain mapping; Toronto Public Health Heat Vulnerability Index.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: climate risk assessment completed within 5 years; resilience plan in place for high-risk facilities. Poor: facility in high-risk zone with no current assessment or plan.
Availability/proxy: TRCA flood maps and TPH heat vulnerability index available publicly. Proxy: facility location in TRCA-designated floodplain.

Domain: Parks — access and programming
Facility type: Parks
Metric: Parkland per capita
Definition: Total parkland area (ha) ÷ catchment population. Unit: ha per 1,000 residents.
Rationale: Core Toronto service standard; inequitable parkland distribution is a well-documented equity issue in Toronto's inner suburbs vs downtown.
Data sources: Toronto Parks Forestry and Recreation (PF&R) parkland inventory; Statistics Canada Census population.
Benchmarks/thresholds: City of Toronto policy goal is 1.5–2.0 ha per 1,000 residents (varies by planning district). Good: ≥1.5 ha/1,000. Poor: <0.8 ha/1,000. Derived from Toronto Official Plan parkland provisions.
Availability/proxy: PF&R publishes parkland area data. Proxy: park area per square kilometre by ward from Toronto Open Data.

---

Domain: Parks — access and programming
Facility type: Parks
Metric: Active amenity density and condition
Definition: Count and % of amenities (sports courts, playgrounds, splash pads, sports fields, benches, shade structures) in good condition per hectare of parkland. Unit: amenities/ha; % in good condition.
Rationale: Amenity availability and condition directly drives park use; poor condition of playgrounds is a safety and equity issue.
Data sources: Toronto PF&R Parks Condition Assessment; PF&R capital planning database.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥80% of amenities rated 'good' or 'excellent' in most recent condition assessment. Poor: >25% of amenities rated 'poor' or 'critical.'
Availability/proxy: Condition assessment data partially public in PF&R SOGR reports. Proxy: 311 park maintenance service requests per hectare.

---

Domain: Parks — access and programming
Facility type: Parks
Metric: Tree canopy cover (%)
Definition: % of park area covered by tree canopy. Unit: %.
Rationale: Toronto's Urban Forest Strategy targets 40% city-wide canopy cover; parks are primary contributors; canopy provides cooling, biodiversity, and mental health benefits.
Data sources: Toronto Urban Forest inventory and canopy cover mapping (Toronto Open Data).
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥40% canopy in park area (consistent with city strategy). Poor: <20%, especially in heat-vulnerable neighbourhoods.
Availability/proxy: Canopy data on Toronto Open Data. Proxy: # of dead/hazard trees relative to total tree inventory in park.

---

Domain: Parks — access and programming
Facility type: Parks
Metric: Activation programming hours
Definition: Total hours per year of organized programming (City-run or permitted community programming) per hectare of parkland. Unit: hours/ha/year.
Rationale: 'Dead' parks with no programming attract less use and create perceived safety concerns; activation is a key strategy for equity-deserving communities.
Data sources: Toronto PF&R programming schedules; park permit records.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Benchmark via peer parks of similar size and neighbourhood NES. Good: above peer median, with programming reaching diverse demographics. Poor: below peer median with no equity-targeted programming.
Availability/proxy: Permit data partially public. Proxy: count of posted City programming and community events per park per year.

---

Domain: Parks — access and programming
Facility type: Parks
Metric: Maintenance response time (311 service standard compliance)
Definition: % of park maintenance service requests (from 311 or staff inspection) resolved within City service standard timeframe by category (e.g., graffiti: 2 business days in high-visibility areas; broken equipment: 24h if safety-critical). Unit: %.
Rationale: Maintenance responsiveness is a direct equity signal — research shows high-NES parks receive faster service; this must be tracked and equalized.
Data sources: 311 Toronto service request data (Toronto Open Data); PF&R service standard tracking.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥90% of service requests resolved within standard. Poor: <75% or material difference in compliance between NES quintiles.
Availability/proxy: 311 data on Toronto Open Data allows analysis by park location and ward.

chunks.section: Toronto Public Library: access and service
chunks.text:
Domain: Libraries — access and service
Facility type: Toronto Public Library (TPL) branches
Metric: Visits per capita (catchment)
Definition: Annual branch visits ÷ catchment population. Unit: visits per person.
Rationale: Primary utilization measure; TPL tracks this as a core performance indicator; low rates signal access barriers or programming mismatch.
Data sources: Toronto Public Library Annual Report; TPL Open Data (visits by branch); Statistics Canada Census.
Benchmarks/thresholds: TPL system average and branch distribution. Good: above system-average visits/capita. Poor: bottom quartile — triggers service review.
Availability/proxy: TPL publishes branch-level visit data on Open Data.

---

Domain: Libraries — access and service
Facility type: Toronto Public Library branches
Metric: Active cardholders as % of catchment population
Definition: # active cardholders with address in catchment ÷ catchment population. Unit: %.
Rationale: Cardholder penetration measures community attachment to the branch; low rates in high-need areas suggest registration friction or relevance gap.
Data sources: TPL cardholder database by postal code; Statistics Canada Census.
Benchmarks/thresholds: TPL system average benchmark. Good: ≥ system average. Poor: <50% of system average in NES <40 catchment — triggers outreach review.
Availability/proxy: TPL publishes aggregate cardholder data. Branch-level proxy: registered borrower counts in annual report.

---

Domain: Libraries — access and service
Facility type: Toronto Public Library branches
Metric: Digital equity access (internet sessions and device loans)
Definition: # public internet sessions + device loans per year per branch; % of sessions by unhoused or marginally-housed users (where collected). Unit: count; %.
Rationale: Libraries are Toronto's most equitable digital access infrastructure; digital equity service intensity is a key equity performance indicator.
Data sources: TPL Digital Access Report; Toronto Open Data branch-level usage.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: internet and device access available all operating hours; digital literacy programming ≥2 sessions/week. Poor: no public internet or device lending program.
Availability/proxy: TPL Annual Report and Open Data.

---

Domain: Libraries — access and service
Facility type: Toronto Public Library branches
Metric: Collection recency and relevance
Definition: % of circulating collection items published within last 5 years; % of collection in languages other than English proportionate to catchment multilingual population. Unit: %.
Rationale: Outdated or language-inappropriate collections reduce relevance and utilization, especially in immigrant-dense catchments.
Data sources: TPL collection management system; Census language data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥50% of circulating items published within 5 years; multilingual collection ≥ proportionate to catchment need. Poor: <30% current items; <50% of needed multilingual representation.
Availability/proxy: TPL Annual Report provides aggregate metrics; proxy: circulation-to-item ratio by language group.

---

Domain: Libraries — access and service
Facility type: Toronto Public Library branches
Metric: Social service navigation / warm referral capacity
Definition: Presence of trained Community Connector or social worker; # warm referrals to social services per year. Unit: binary + count.
Rationale: Toronto libraries serve as de facto social infrastructure for unhoused, newcomer, and marginalized residents; formal referral capacity is an equity imperative.
Data sources: TPL Community Connector program records; branch service plan.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: dedicated community navigation staff in all NES <40 branches; referral tracking in place. Poor: no social service navigation capacity in high-need branches.
Availability/proxy: TPL publishes program presence by branch in annual report.

Domain: Community centres — programming and activation
Facility type: Toronto community centres (City-run and non-profit operated under agreement)
Metric: Program registration fill rate
Definition: # registrations ÷ total program capacity. Unit: %.
Rationale: Fill rate measures programming relevance and marketing effectiveness; both over- and under-fill are performance signals.
Data sources: ActiveTO registration system; divisional service reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: 70–90% fill rate across programming mix. Poor: <50% fill rate sustained (investigate barriers) or >95% sustained (capacity constraint).
Availability/proxy: City publishes aggregate recreation data. Proxy: waitlist length per program type.

---

Domain: Community centres — programming and activation
Facility type: Toronto community centres
Metric: Fee assistance utilization rate (Recreation Fee Assistance)
Definition: # residents using City Recreation Fee Assistance at this centre ÷ estimated eligible residents in catchment. Unit: %.
Rationale: Measures reach of equity programming; low uptake in high-NES-need catchments indicates awareness, registration, or stigma barriers.
Data sources: City of Toronto Recreation Fee Assistance database; Census income data by catchment.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: uptake rising year-over-year; fee assistance users proportionate to eligible population. Poor: <25% of estimated eligible population accessing subsidy.
Availability/proxy: Fee assistance aggregate data in City budget and service reports.

---

Domain: Community centres — programming and activation
Facility type: Toronto community centres
Metric: Space utilization rate (room/studio/gym bookings)
Definition: Total programmed or booked hours ÷ total available programmed hours. Unit: %.
Rationale: Under-utilized space represents waste of a scarce public asset; over-utilization prevents community access.
Data sources: Facility booking system; divisional space management reports.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: 65–85% space utilization (allowing for maintenance and setup time). Poor: <45% (investigate demand mismatch) or >95% with waitlisted community groups.
Availability/proxy: Internal booking data. Proxy: # of community group booking rejections per year.

---

Domain: Community centres — programming and activation
Facility type: Toronto community centres
Metric: Youth and seniors program reach
Definition: # unique youth (under 18) and seniors (65+) participants per year as % of youth and senior population in catchment. Unit: %.
Rationale: Community centres are a primary social infrastructure for Toronto's youth and seniors; low reach signals programming, awareness, or access failure.
Data sources: ActiveTO registration data by age; Statistics Canada Census age data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥20% of catchment youth and ≥15% of catchment seniors participating annually. Poor: <10% for either group.
Availability/proxy: Registration data available to City. Proxy: count of youth and senior programming hours per week.

---

Domain: Community centres — programming and activation
Facility type: Toronto community centres
Metric: Aquatics safety record (if applicable)
Definition: # serious aquatic incidents (near-drownings, rescues, injuries requiring medical attention) per 100,000 swims. Unit: rate.
Rationale: Aquatic facilities carry the highest safety risk of any community centre space; mandatory public health and insurance reporting standards apply.
Data sources: Toronto Public Health pool inspection records; City risk management incident data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: zero serious incidents; pool inspections pass rate 100%. Poor: any serious incident or failed health inspection not corrected within required timeframe.
Availability/proxy: TPH pool inspection results publicly available.

chunks.section: Transit: TTC and Metrolinx facility evaluation
chunks.text:
Domain: Transportation infrastructure — transit facilities
Facility type: TTC stations, bus terminals, Metrolinx GO stations, cycling infrastructure
Metric: On-time performance (OTP)
Definition: % of scheduled vehicle departures departing within defined threshold (TTC: within 3 minutes; GO Transit: within 5 minutes). Unit: %.
Rationale: Core service reliability metric; directly affects commuter experience and equity of access to employment, healthcare, and services.
Data sources: TTC CEO's Report (monthly); Metrolinx GO Transit Performance Dashboard; Toronto Open Data.
Benchmarks/thresholds: TTC surface routes target: ≥75% OTP; subway: ≥90%. GO Transit: ≥92%. Good: at or above target. Poor: >5 percentage points below target.
Availability/proxy: Both TTC and Metrolinx publish OTP publicly.

---

Domain: Transportation infrastructure — transit facilities
Facility type: TTC/Metrolinx station infrastructure
Metric: Elevator and escalator availability rate (accessibility critical)
Definition: % of scheduled operating hours that elevators and escalators are in service (not unplanned out-of-service). Unit: %.
Rationale: Elevator outages in TTC stations eliminate access for people with mobility disabilities, parents with strollers, and older adults; TTC has a legal AODA obligation.
Data sources: TTC Elevator and Escalator Status (real-time and historical via TTC Open Data); TTC CEO's Report.
Benchmarks/thresholds: TTC target: ≥97% elevator availability. Good: ≥97%. Poor: <90% or any station with no elevator alternative during outage.
Availability/proxy: TTC publishes elevator status data on Open Data.

---

Domain: Transportation infrastructure — transit facilities
Facility type: TTC/Metrolinx station infrastructure
Metric: Station safety and personal security incidents per million boardings
Definition: Reported safety and personal security incidents (assaults, robberies, threats) ÷ annual boardings × 1,000,000. Unit: rate per million boardings.
Rationale: Station safety is a key determinant of transit usage, particularly by women, youth, and marginalized groups; trending rate informs security investment decisions.
Data sources: TTC CEO's Report (monthly safety dashboard); Toronto Police Service calls for service by location.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Benchmark via TTC system average and trend. Good: at or below system average rate and declining. Poor: >2x system average rate or sustained increase.
Availability/proxy: TTC safety data in CEO's Report. Proxy: Toronto Police calls for service by station (Toronto Open Data).

---

Domain: Transportation infrastructure — transit facilities
Facility type: Transit and active transportation infrastructure
Metric: Cycling infrastructure coverage and quality
Definition: % of streets in catchment with protected or separated cycling infrastructure; condition rating of existing cycling lanes. Unit: %; condition score.
Rationale: Toronto's Cycling Network Plan commits to building a connected cycling network; gaps disproportionately affect lower-income wards that depend more on active transportation.
Data sources: City of Toronto cycling network data (Toronto Open Data); PF&R trail condition assessments.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥50% of arterial streets in catchment have protected cycling infrastructure (Toronto cycling strategy goal). Poor: <20% coverage in high-cycling-demand corridors.
Availability/proxy: Cycling network data on Toronto Open Data.

---

Domain: Transportation infrastructure — transit facilities
Facility type: All transit infrastructure
Metric: Transit equity index (transit access by income quintile)
Definition: Average frequency and speed of transit service accessible within 800m of residences, disaggregated by income quintile of neighbourhood. Unit: composite score.
Rationale: Toronto has well-documented transit inequity (inner-suburb residents with lower incomes have worse transit access); this metric directly tracks the equity gap.
Data sources: Metrolinx/TTC GTFS schedule data; Statistics Canada Census income data; Walk Score / TransitScore APIs.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: transit score gap between highest- and lowest-income quintiles <15 points. Poor: gap >30 points or widening trend.
Availability/proxy: GTFS data publicly available; Metrolinx Regional Transportation Plan equity reports.

Toronto's acute-care hospital sector is among the most complex in Canada, encompassing major academic health sciences centres (University Health Network, Sinai Health, Unity Health, Toronto Metropolitan University-affiliated sites), large community hospitals, and specialty facilities. The following Toronto-specific contextual factors must inform any hospital evaluation:

1. Academic mission adjustment: Toronto's downtown hospitals disproportionately carry teaching and research missions that inflate cost per case, case-mix weight (RIW), and administrative overhead. All cost and outcome comparisons must use teaching-hospital peer groups, not all-Ontario averages.

2. Social complexity of catchment: Downtown Toronto hospitals serve populations with extremely high rates of homelessness, mental health and substance use (MHSU) conditions, immigration, and linguistic diversity. ED utilization and readmission rates must be risk-adjusted for social complexity (CIHI's Johns Hopkins ACG case-mix is the recommended tool for social complexity adjustment).

3. Toronto Central LHIN (now Ontario Health Toronto Region) performance context: hospitals are embedded in a regional system with shared targets for ED flow, ALC reduction, and MHSU follow-up. Evaluate individual hospital performance within regional system performance.

4. Key public data sources specific to Toronto hospitals:
   - Ontario Health / HQO hospital public reporting portal
   - CIHI indicator library (HSMR, readmissions, hospital harm, ALC)
   - Ontario patient safety public reporting (CDI, MRSA/VRE, hand hygiene, surgical checklist)
   - Hospital Quality Improvement Plans (QIPs) — mandatory annual public disclosure
   - Hospital Service Accountability Agreements (H-SAAs) — financial and service commitments
   - Toronto Public Health population health profiles for hospital service area context

5. Equity data gap: Toronto hospitals serve the most diverse population in Canada, yet race-based and language-stratified outcome data remain inconsistently collected. The Ontario Health Equity Data Standard (2022) requires collection of race, Indigenous identity, and preferred language; compliance and utilization of this data for equity reporting must be explicitly evaluated.

chunks.section: Hospital: clinical quality, access, and safety
chunks.text:
Domain: Hospital — clinical quality and outcomes
Facility type: Acute-care hospitals (Toronto)
Metric: Hospital Standardized Mortality Ratio (HSMR)
Definition: Actual in-hospital deaths ÷ expected deaths × 100 (risk-adjusted for age, sex, diagnosis, comorbidities, admission urgency). Unit: index.
Rationale: High-level outcome screening signal for patient safety; <100 indicates better-than-expected mortality.
Data sources: CIHI HSMR indicator; published in HQO/Ontario Health hospital profiles.
Benchmarks/thresholds: 100 = national average. Good: significantly <100 (with CI testing). Poor: significantly >100. Always compare to teaching-hospital peer group for Toronto downtown hospitals.
Availability/proxy: Published publicly for most Ontario hospitals. Proxy: condition-specific 30-day mortality (stroke, AMI).

---

Domain: Hospital — access and flow
Facility type: Acute-care hospitals (Toronto)
Metric: ED length of stay — non-admitted, 90th percentile
Definition: Time from triage/registration to departure for patients not admitted. 90th percentile hours. Unit: hours.
Rationale: Core ED access metric; Ontario has explicit provincial targets.
Data sources: Ontario Health ED public reporting (NACRS-based).
Benchmarks/thresholds: Low-urgency target: ≤4 hours. High-urgency target: ≤8 hours at 90th percentile. Good: ≥90% within target. Poor: materially below target.
Availability/proxy: Public by hospital.

---

Domain: Hospital — safety and infection control
Facility type: Acute-care hospitals (Toronto)
Metric: Hospital-associated C. difficile (CDI) rate
Definition: New hospital-associated CDI cases ÷ patient days × 1,000. Unit: rate per 1,000 patient days.
Rationale: Core healthcare-associated infection (HAI) marker; Ontario mandatory public reporting.
Data sources: HQO patient safety public reporting; Public Health Ontario HAI query.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: below provincial average and improving. Poor: above provincial average or outbreak-linked elevations.
Availability/proxy: Publicly reported; small-count suppression applies.

---

Domain: Hospital — equity and community integration
Facility type: Acute-care hospitals (Toronto)
Metric: 7-day mental health follow-up after discharge
Definition: % psychiatric discharges with community follow-up visit within 7 days. Unit: %.
Rationale: High-value integration measure critical in Toronto's context of high MHSU burden; strong predictor of readmission reduction.
Data sources: Ontario Health indicator (public reporting); CIHI mental health indicators.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: top quartile vs Ontario peers and improving. Poor: bottom quartile or declining trend.
Availability/proxy: Publicly reported in some contexts. Proxy: 30-day MHSU readmission rate.

---

Domain: Hospital — workforce
Facility type: Acute-care hospitals (Toronto)
Metric: Nurse-to-patient ratio (med-surg, unit-specific)
Definition: Average patient assignment per RN by unit and shift (med-surg day, night; ICU; ED). Unit: patients per RN.
Rationale: Directly associated with mortality, adverse events, and staff burnout; must be acuity-adjusted.
Data sources: Internal hospital staffing systems; union disclosures; nurse-worked-hours-per-weighted-case (CIHI MIS where available).
Benchmarks/thresholds: Evidence-based: med-surg >5–6 patients/RN is 'high risk' threshold. ICU: 1:1–2 standard. Good: at or below professionally recognized ratios with no sustained deviation. Poor: sustained above evidence-based thresholds with worsening outcomes.
Availability/proxy: Rarely public. Proxy: nursing worked hours per weighted case (CIHI) + agency spend + overtime rate.

Domain: Governance and accountability
Metric: Strategic plan currency and alignment
Definition: Whether the facility or division has a current strategic/service plan (≤5 years old) aligned to Toronto's key strategic plans (Toronto Strong Neighbourhoods, Toronto Poverty Reduction Strategy, TransformTO, Toronto Seniors Strategy). Unit: binary + alignment score.
Rationale: Strategic alignment ensures facility priorities reflect city-wide equity and sustainability goals, not siloed operational logic.
Data sources: Divisional service plans; City Clerk's strategic document registry.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: current plan with documented links to ≥3 city-wide strategies. Poor: no current plan or plan >5 years old.
Availability/proxy: Divisional plans publicly available on City website.

---

Domain: Governance and accountability
Metric: Quality Improvement Plan (QIP) completion rate
Definition: % of QIP commitments from prior year that were completed or substantially progressed. Unit: %.
Rationale: QIPs (mandatory for Ontario hospitals; best practice for other City facilities) drive structured improvement; completion rate signals execution capacity.
Data sources: Hospital QIPs (mandatory public reporting via Ontario Health); divisional improvement plans.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥80% of prior-year QIP targets met. Poor: <50% or targets not publicly reported.
Availability/proxy: Hospital QIPs publicly available. Other City facilities: proxy via service plan target tracking.

---

Domain: Governance and accountability
Metric: Public transparency and open data participation
Definition: % of core performance indicators published on Toronto Open Data or equivalent public portal; date of last update. Unit: %; days since last update.
Rationale: Transparency is a prerequisite for public accountability; Toronto's open data commitment requires city facilities to publish performance data.
Data sources: Toronto Open Data portal; facility/division public reporting pages.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: ≥70% of core KPIs publicly available and updated within past 12 months. Poor: <30% publicly available or data >24 months stale.
Availability/proxy: Toronto Open Data portal (open.toronto.ca).

---

Domain: Governance and accountability
Metric: Audit and oversight findings
Definition: # material findings from Auditor General, Ontario Ombudsman, or City internal audit in past 3 years; % of recommendations implemented. Unit: count; %.
Rationale: Independent audit findings signal governance quality; low implementation of recommendations signals accountability failure.
Data sources: City of Toronto Auditor General reports (public); Ontario Ombudsman reports; City internal audit summaries.
Benchmarks/thresholds: Good: zero material audit findings or ≥90% of prior recommendations implemented. Poor: repeat material findings or <60% recommendation implementation.
Availability/proxy: Auditor General reports publicly available on City website.

chunks.domain: Toronto data ecosystem and key sources
chunks.section: Toronto data ecosystem: key sources by facility type
chunks.text:
Key Toronto-specific data sources for civic facility evaluation:

UNIVERSAL (ALL FACILITY TYPES):
- Toronto Open Data portal (open.toronto.ca): facility locations, 311 service requests, budget data, workforce data, energy benchmarking, cycling network, tree canopy.
- City of Toronto Neighbourhood Equity Score (NES): neighbourhood-level equity index for contextualizing facility performance.
- Toronto Public Health Neighbourhood Profiles: population health indicators, social determinants, and equity metrics by neighbourhood.
- Statistics Canada Census (by FSA and Census Tract): population, language, income, age, disability, immigration status.
- City of Toronto Capital Budget and State of Good Repair (SOGR) backlog reports: infrastructure investment and condition data.
- City of Toronto Auditor General reports: independent performance and accountability assessments.
- City of Toronto Workforce Census: staff diversity and representation data (every 3 years).

PARKS:
- Toronto Parks Forestry and Recreation (PF&R) divisional reports and condition assessments.
- Toronto Urban Forest inventory and canopy cover map (Toronto Open Data).
- 311 service request data filtered by park-related categories.
- Toronto and Region Conservation Authority (TRCA) mapping for natural areas and flood risk.

LIBRARIES:
- Toronto Public Library Annual Report (tpl.ca/annual-report).
- TPL Open Data: branch visits, cardholders, program registrations, digital access.
- TPL Strategic Plan and Equity, Diversity and Inclusion reports.

COMMUNITY CENTRES:
- City of Toronto Parks, Forestry and Recreation divisional service reports.
- ActiveTO registration system data (aggregate available in service reports).
- Toronto Recreation Fee Assistance program utilization reports.
- Toronto Public Health pool and food service inspection records.

TRANSPORTATION:
- TTC CEO's Report (monthly, public): ridership, OTP, safety, accessibility, elevator availability.
- TTC Open Data: GTFS schedules, elevator/escalator status, ridership by station.
- Metrolinx GO Transit Performance Dashboard.
- City of Toronto Cycling Network Plan progress reports and cycling data (Open Data).
- Toronto Police Service calls for service by location (Open Data).

HOSPITALS:
- Ontario Health / HQO hospital public reporting portal (hqontario.ca).
- CIHI Indicator Library: HSMR, readmissions, hospital harm, ALC, occupancy.
- Ontario patient safety public reporting: CDI, MRSA/VRE bacteremia, hand hygiene, surgical safety checklist.
- Hospital Quality Improvement Plans (QIPs) — mandatory annual public disclosure via Ontario Health.
- Public Health Ontario HAI Query Tool.
- Hospital Service Accountability Agreements (H-SAAs) — financial and service commitments.
- Ontario Health Equity Data Standard (2022) — race-based and sociodemographic data collection standard.
- Ontario Case Costing Initiative (OCCI) — hospital cost data.
